unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you learn to count in tens all the way to one hundred. You name the shapes circle, square, triangle, rectangle and heart, and you spot patterns that repeat. Best of all, you learn to measure and compare: which is bigger, which is longer, which is taller and which is heavier. You measure with a ruler in centimetres and metres, ask “How many…?” and answer with There is or There are, and you sort out the number words that sound like other words. Measuring carefully helps us understand the world and be fair to everyone, just like Amal and her friends in this unit's story, “A Fair Way to Measure”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few vocabulary words each day from the four groups: counting in tens, shapes and patterns, measuring words and comparing words.
Work through the six grammar practices: comparing with -er than, asking How many with There is and There are, and the number words that sound the same.
Read and listen to the four texts and the story “A Fair Way to Measure”, then answer the nineteen comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 5 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md
unit.reviewStatus: Pilot rebuild - pending curriculum reviewer sign-off

visual.alt: Cartoon children in a school yard measuring a floor mat with a metre stick, with circles, squares, triangles and rectangles drawn around them.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 10, which is one whole group of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are ten fingers on my two hands.
practiceSentences: Amal put ten beans into the first little cup.
Can you count all the way to ten without stopping?
“Make one group of ten first,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I can hop ten times before my legs get tired.
Our ruler shows ten centimetres before the first long mark.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, make a group of ten beans, then ask the tutor to check the sentence you say about it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 20, which is two groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Twenty children are playing in the field.
practiceSentences: Twenty chairs stand in a long row in our hall.
How quickly can you count from ten to twenty?
“I have twenty bottle caps in my box,” said Leo.
I counted twenty steps from the gate to the tree.
The class found twenty circles on the shape chart.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - w - e - n - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to say a number between ten and twenty, then say the next ten after it while your helper listens.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 30, which is three groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are thirty days in the month of September.
practiceSentences: Thirty beans filled the small blue jar to the top.
Did you really count thirty windows in our school?
“Stop at thirty and take a breath,” said Nora.
I can count in tens up to thirty without any help.
The rope in the school yard is thirty metres long.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - h - i - r - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Count to thirty in tens for the tutor with your helper listening, then ask it to clap for every ten you say.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 40, which is four groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The farmer has forty goats.
practiceSentences: Forty birds landed on the roof beside the market.
Can you write forty in words and in numerals?
“We still need forty more beans,” said Sami quietly.
My grandmother gave me forty tiny buttons to count.
Leo jumped forty centimetres further than he did yesterday.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to spell forty slowly while your helper watches, then write it and read it back.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 50, which is five groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Fifty people came to the school fair.
practiceSentences: Fifty pencils stood in the tall pot on the desk.
Is fifty more than forty or less than forty?
“I counted fifty shapes on the chart,” said Nora.
I clapped fifty times while my sister counted for me.
The mat in the reading corner is fifty centimetres wide.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say fifty and then fifteen for the tutor, with an adult listening, and ask it which ending it heard each time.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 60, which is six groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are sixty minutes in one hour.
practiceSentences: Sixty seeds waited in a neat pile on the tray.
How long does sixty seconds feel when you stand still?
“Count on to sixty,” said Teacher Yasmin with a smile.
I can say the tens all the way up to sixty.
The long ribbon measures sixty centimetres from end to end.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper timing you, count to sixty for the tutor and ask it whether your counting stayed steady.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 70, which is seven groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My grandfather is seventy years old.
practiceSentences: Seventy tiny stones lay in the corner of the yard.
Which number comes just after seventy in our counting song?
“Seventy is one more ten than sixty,” explained Maya.
I have counted seventy steps from my door to school.
The garden wall is seventy centimetres high.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - v - e - n - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to start counting in tens and stop at seventy, then carry on yourself while an adult checks.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 80, which is eight groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The old tree in our school yard is eighty years old.
practiceSentences: Eighty leaves floated slowly down onto the school path.
Can you count backwards from eighty to seventy?
“We have reached eighty now,” called Daniel from the door.
I coloured eighty squares on my number chart today.
The trunk of the shady tree measures eighty centimetres around.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - i - g - h - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Count backwards from eighty to ten for the tutor while your helper sits with you, and ask it to spot any number you skip.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 90, which is nine groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She scored ninety points in the game.
practiceSentences: Ninety beads shone in the bowl on the window sill.
What number comes after ninety when we count in tens?
“Only ninety left to go,” laughed Theo.
I counted ninety claps before my hands got tired.
The playground fence is ninety centimetres tall.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - i - n - e - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor for one more ten after ninety, with an adult beside you, and explain how you knew the answer.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The number 100, which is ten whole groups of ten ones.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I can count to one hundred by tens!
practiceSentences: One hundred centimetres make one whole metre.
Can you reach one hundred by counting in tens?
“There are one hundred centimetres in one metre,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I sang the counting song until I reached one hundred.
One hundred little fingers waved high in the air.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - n - e - h - u - n - d - r - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Count from ten to one hundred in tens for the tutor while your adult helper listens, then ask it how many tens you said.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A round shape with no corners and no straight sides.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sun looks like a circle in the sky.
practiceSentences: The clock face on our classroom wall is a circle.
Can you draw a circle without lifting your pencil?
“Sit in a circle for story time,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I traced round a cup to make my circle neat.
Every wheel on the market cart is a circle.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - i - r - c - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, name three round things in your room and ask the tutor whether each one is really a circle.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A shape with four straight sides that are all the same length.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The window has a square shape.
practiceSentences: Each tile on the kitchen floor is a perfect square.
How many corners can you count on a square?
“My sandwich is cut into one big square,” said Leo.
I folded my paper until it made a small square.
The mat in the reading corner is almost a square.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - q - u - a - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain the difference between a square and a rectangle, then say it back in your own words with your helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A shape with three straight sides and three pointed corners.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The roof has a triangle shape.
practiceSentences: The slice of watermelon on my plate is a triangle.
Which shape has fewer sides, a triangle or a square?
“Look, that roof is a triangle,” said Nora, pointing.
I made a triangle with three short sticks from the garden.
Our sports flag is a bright red triangle.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - i - a - n - g - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Draw a triangle while an adult watches, count its sides aloud, and ask the tutor to check your counting.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A shape with four sides, where the two sides opposite each other match.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The door has a rectangle shape.
practiceSentences: My reading book is a rectangle with two long sides.
Is your desk a rectangle or a square?
“The door of our class is a tall rectangle,” said Sami.
I drew a rectangle around my best piece of writing.
The blackboard is a wide rectangle at the front.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - c - t - a - n - g - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Find one rectangle in your home with your helper, then ask the tutor which of its sides should be the same length.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A shape with two round tops and one point at the bottom.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She drew a heart on the front of her card.
practiceSentences: Maya cut a red heart out of thick paper.
Can you draw a heart with one smooth line?
“I put a heart on my card,” said Nora happily.
I made a heart from ten small blue beads.
A heart pattern runs along the edge of the cloth.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Draw a heart beside your adult helper and ask the tutor to describe the shape back to you in two sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A design that repeats the same thing again and again.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The cloth has a pattern of squares and circles.
practiceSentences: Our pattern goes circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle.
What comes next in this pattern of shapes?
“Say the pattern out loud with me,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I made a pattern with red and blue bottle caps.
The fence has a pattern of tall and short posts.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - a - t - t - e - r - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say your repeating pattern to the tutor with an adult listening, and ask it to guess what comes next.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To find out how long, how tall or how heavy something is.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We measure the mat with a metre stick.
practiceSentences: The children measure the classroom floor slowly and carefully.
Can you measure your desk with a ruler?
“Let us measure the space for the mat,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I like to measure my shoes and write down the number.
We measure water in a jug and cloth with a tape.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - a - s - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Measure one object with an adult beside you, then ask the tutor whether you should record centimetres or metres.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A flat tool with marks on it for measuring and drawing straight lines.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I draw a line with my ruler.
practiceSentences: Leo keeps his ruler flat inside his pencil case.
Where did you put the long wooden ruler?
“Hold the ruler still,” said Nora, pointing at the mark.
My ruler shows thirty centimetres from one end to the other.
A ruler gives the same answer for everybody in the class.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Show your helper where the zero mark sits on your ruler, then ask the tutor why measuring must start there.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small unit of length; one hundred of them make one metre.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The shell is 3 centimetres long.
practiceSentences: My little finger is about five centimetres long.
How many centimetres wide is your reading book?
“It measures forty centimetres,” said Sami, checking again.
I use centimetres whenever I measure something small.
One centimetre is much shorter than one metre.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - e - n - t - i - m - e - t - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Measure your hand in centimetres with an adult helping, then ask the tutor to compare your answer with a ruler length.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A bigger unit of length that is one hundred centimetres long.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The floor is three metres long.
practiceSentences: The reading corner is three metres from wall to wall.
Is your bed longer than one metre?
“A metre is always the same length,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I use a metre stick whenever I measure a whole room.
We measure a long road in metres, not in centimetres.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name three things that are about one metre long, then check one of them with your adult helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How long something is from one end to the other end.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We measured the length of the table.
practiceSentences: The length of the red ribbon is sixty centimetres.
Which has the greater length, your arm or your leg?
“Write the length in your book,” said Maya.
I found the length of my desk with a ruler.
We wrote the length of every shelf onto a chart.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - e - n - g - t - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor the length of two objects you measured with your helper, and ask which one it thinks is longer.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How tall something is from the bottom to the top.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The height of the chair is 40 centimetres.
practiceSentences: The height of the shady tree surprised the whole class.
Do you know the height of your bedroom door?
“Stand straight so I can mark your height,” said Nora.
I check my height against the wall every birthday.
The height of the fence keeps the small goats inside.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to mark your height on paper, then ask the tutor how you could write that height in centimetres.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How heavy or how light something is when you lift it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The big basket has more weight than the small one.
practiceSentences: The weight of the full basket made Sami stop and rest.
Can you guess the weight of this bag of beans?
“Share the weight between us,” said Maya kindly.
I felt the weight of the wet towel in my hands.
A feather has almost no weight at all.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - e - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Hold two objects with your adult helper and tell the tutor which weight feels greater, then explain how you decided.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How big or how small something is.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: These two shoes are a different size.
practiceSentences: Every cushion in the reading corner is a different size.
What size of paper do you need for your chart?
“These two boxes are the same size,” said Daniel.
I wear a bigger size of shoe than my sister.
The size of the mat must suit six friends.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Put three objects in order of size with an adult watching, then ask the tutor to check your order.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Large in size; it takes up a lot of space.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The elephant at the zoo is very big.
practiceSentences: Sami lifted the big basket with both of his arms.
Is your school bag big enough for all your books?
“That big cushion needs the longer ribbon,” said Daniel.
I sat on the big mat with five of my friends.
A big shape is easy to see from far away.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Point to the biggest thing in your room while an adult sits with you, and ask the tutor to describe it back.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Little in size; it does not take up much space.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A mouse is small next to an elephant.
practiceSentences: Maya carried the small basket to the reading corner.
Can a small stone be heavier than a big leaf?
“My hands are too small for this jar,” said Leo.
I keep my small coins inside a round tin box.
The small window lets in only a little light.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Find the smallest object on your table with your helper, then ask the tutor for two words that describe it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Measuring a lot from one end to the other end.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A snake is long and thin.
practiceSentences: The red ribbon is long enough to tie the big cushion.
How long is the mat in the reading corner?
“This long metre stick will help us,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I have a long pencil and a very short rubber.
The market street is long and busy in the morning.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Measure the longest thing on your desk with an adult, then ask the tutor how to say the length in a full sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Not long; it measures only a little from one end to the other.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A worm is short next to a snake.
practiceSentences: The green ribbon was too short for the big cushion.
Is your pencil short because you have used it often?
“Take the short piece of string,” said Daniel to Maya.
I wrote a short list of the things we measured.
The short walk to the market takes only ten minutes.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Lay two strings side by side with your helper and ask the tutor which one you should call the short one.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: High from the bottom to the top, like a tree or a person.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The tree in the school yard is tall.
practiceSentences: Theo stood up straight to show how tall he is.
How tall are you in centimetres this year?
“Being tall does not mean you go first,” said Amal.
I want to be as tall as my big brother.
The tall shelf holds all of our reading books.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Stand back to back with an adult, then ask the tutor how to write a sentence saying who is tall.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Not tall; it does not reach far up from the ground.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bush by the gate is short.
practiceSentences: The little bush beside the gate is short and round.
Are you taller or shorter than your best friend?
“I am short, so I sit at the front,” said Nora.
I stood on a short stool to reach the top shelf.
The short fence keeps the small goats in the garden.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain why we say short for a bush and also for a pencil, with your helper listening.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Hard to lift because it weighs a lot.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A big stone is heavy to lift.
practiceSentences: The basket of cushions was heavy in Sami's arms.
Is this bag too heavy for you to carry?
“Let me help with the heavy basket,” said Maya.
I could not lift the heavy box of metre sticks alone.
Wet clothes feel heavy when you take them off the line.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Lift two bags with an adult holding one, then ask the tutor how to compare them using the word heavier.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Easy to lift because it does not weigh very much.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A feather is light and floats in the air.
practiceSentences: The small basket was light, so Sami carried it easily.
Which is light, a paper cup or a full jug?
“This one is light,” said Sami, lifting the smaller basket.
I chose the light bag because my arms were tired.
A light box can still be big and wide.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor for three things that are light enough for you to lift, then test one with your helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Big from one side across to the other side.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The river is wide.
practiceSentences: The classroom door is wide enough for two children.
How wide is the mat in the reading corner?
“The wide shelf holds all the boxes,” said Nora.
I measured the wide table with our longest ruler.
The market road is wide and full of carts.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Measure across a table with an adult, then ask the tutor whether wide or long is the better word for that number.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Thin from one side across to the other side.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The path is narrow.
practiceSentences: The narrow path behind the school fits only one child.
Is this shelf too narrow for your big books?
“Walk slowly on the narrow step,” said Maya.
I chose the narrow ribbon for my small card.
A narrow gap in the fence lets the cat through.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - a - r - r - o - w
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Point to the narrowest gap in your room while your helper watches, and ask the tutor to describe it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Far up above the ground.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bird is high above our shady tree.
practiceSentences: The nest sat high in the branches of the tree.
How high can you hold your hands in the air?
“Put the box on the high shelf,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I raised my ten fingers high and counted them all.
The high wall keeps the wind out of our garden.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Reach up with an adult standing near you, then ask the tutor to say your sentence about the high shelf correctly.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Near the ground, not far up.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The nest in the small bush is low.
practiceSentences: The low branch is easy for a small child to reach.
Why is that shelf so low on the wall?
“Sit on the low bench,” said Nora, moving along.
I put my heavy bag down on the low step.
The low fence lets us see the whole garden.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Name one high thing and one low thing to the tutor with your helper beside you, and ask it to check both words.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Count in tens from ten to one hundred, and choose the right number word when two words sound the same.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Ten correct number words in Activity 1, the counting recording in Speaking 1, and five correct choices in Grammar Practice 3.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe the shapes circle, square, triangle, rectangle and heart.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All five shapes named aloud with their number of sides in Speaking 2, plus four correct answers in Activity 2 and four correct matches in Activity 3.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Notice, continue and describe a pattern that repeats.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A repeating pattern described aloud in Speaking 5 and three written sentences in Writing 5 that name the pattern, predict the next part and explain the thinking.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use measuring words such as long, short, tall, heavy, light, wide and narrow.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four measuring words used correctly in the Writing 3 story response, and the measuring words traced through the story in comprehension questions 15 and 17.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Compare two things by adding -er to a describing word and using than.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct -er forms in Grammar Practice 1, four comparing sentences in Activity 4, and two written comparisons in Writing 2.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask “How many…?” and answer with There is or There are.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct choices in Grammar Practice 2, a four-question survey recorded in Speaking 6, and full There is or There are answers in Activity 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Measure length with a ruler, using centimetres and metres.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five objects measured and recorded with the correct unit in Writing 4, and five spoken measurements in the Speaking 4 recording.

readings.title: Shapes All Around Us
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Shapes are all around us. The sun is a circle. Many windows are squares or rectangles. Some roofs are triangles. A clock face is a circle. A book is a rectangle. When we know shapes, we can describe the things we see. We can also make patterns with shapes, like: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle. What shapes do you see around you?

readings.title: A Counting Song
readings.passageScript:
Read and sing this song. Count in tens as you go:
10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers. / 40 little, 50 little, 60 little fingers. / 70 little, 80 little, 90 little fingers. / 100 little fingers in the air!

readings.title: Using Numbers in Class
readings.passageScript:
Listen to this text about children using numbers. Ask an adult to read it aloud, then read it again by yourself.
“The children in Class 2 are using numbers today. Nora is counting shapes on a chart. She counts 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 shapes altogether. Leo is measuring how far he can jump. He jumped 45 centimetres! Amal is looking at the clock. It is 2:30. Numbers help us count, measure and tell the time.”

readings.title: How People Measured Long Ago
readings.passageScript:
Long ago, people did not have rulers. Listen and think:
“Long ago in Egypt, people measured with their fingers, hands and arms. Long ago in Rome, people measured in footsteps. Today we use a ruler and a metre stick, because they are the same for everyone.”

readings.title: A Fair Way to Measure
readings.theme: Measuring and comparing fairly — long, short, tall, big, heavy and light.
readings.setting: A small school beside the town market, with a shady tree in the yard.
readings.passageScript:
Beside the busy town market there was a small school with a shady tree in the yard. One morning, Teacher Yasmin had good news.
“Children, we are going to make a new reading corner,” she said. “We need a mat that is long enough for six friends to sit on. Who can measure the space for me?”
Amal jumped up. “I can measure with my feet!” she said. She walked heel to toe across the floor. “One, two, three … the space is fourteen feet long!”
Leo laughed. “Let me try too.” He walked across the same floor. “One, two, three … I count only eleven feet. You are wrong, Amal!”
“No, you are wrong!” said Amal. The two friends began to argue.
Nora looked down at their feet. “Wait,” she said kindly. “Look. Leo's feet are big. Amal's feet are small. That is why your answers are different. Feet are not a fair way to measure.”
Amal stopped. “You are right, Nora,” she said quietly. “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.”
Teacher Yasmin smiled. “Well done, Nora. Long ago, people measured with their hands and feet. But hands and feet are all different sizes. So today we use a ruler and a metre stick. A metre is always the same length.”
She gave them a long metre stick. “There are one hundred centimetres in one metre,” she said. “Now measure carefully, and be patient.”
The children measured the floor together, slowly and carefully. It was three metres long. Amal wrote the number down. This time, nobody argued.
While they worked, Sami carried two baskets of cushions. “This basket is heavy,” he said, lifting the big one. “And this one is light.” Maya ran to help him carry the heavy basket, so the load was fair for both of them.
Daniel held up two ribbons to tie the cushions. “This red ribbon is longer,” he said. “This green ribbon is shorter. We need the longer one for the big cushion.”
Soon the mat was ready. It looked wonderful. Everyone wanted to sit down first.
“I am the tallest, so I should sit first!” said Theo, standing up straight.
“That is not fair,” said Amal gently. “Being tall does not mean you go first. Let us all sit together.”
Theo thought for a moment. “You are right,” he said. “I am sorry.” He made room, and everyone sat down side by side.
Teacher Yasmin clapped her hands. “Today you learned three important things,” she said. “You learned to measure carefully. You learned to be patient. And you learned to be fair. A fair measure is the same for everyone — and so is a kind heart.”
Amal looked at Leo and smiled. “Your feet are bigger than mine,” she said, “but our mat is the same for both of us.”
Leo laughed. “And it is long enough for all six of us!”
The friends sat on their new mat under the shady tree and opened their books. Outside, the market was noisy and busy. But in the little reading corner, everything felt just right.

comprehension.question: What shape is the sun in this text, and what shape is a book?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The sun is a circle and a book is a rectangle.
comprehension.explanation: Both answers sit in the opening lines: “The sun is a circle” and “A book is a rectangle.”

comprehension.question: The text gives one shape pattern as an example. Write that pattern in the right order.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle.
comprehension.explanation: The pattern is given near the end of the text, and the three shapes repeat in exactly the same order.

comprehension.question: The text says that knowing shapes helps us. What two things does it say we can then do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: We can describe the things we see, and we can make patterns with shapes.
comprehension.explanation: Two sentences at the end of the text give one benefit each, so a full answer names both.

comprehension.question: What is being counted in the counting song?
comprehension.explanation: Every line of the song ends with the word fingers, so fingers are what the singer is counting.

comprehension.question: How many fingers are in the air at the end of the song?
comprehension.correctAnswer: There are one hundred fingers in the air.
comprehension.explanation: The last line of the song reaches one hundred, which is the biggest number in the whole song.

comprehension.question: Each child has ten fingers. How many children would you need to show one hundred fingers?
comprehension.explanation: One hundred is ten groups of ten, so ten children each holding up ten fingers make one hundred.

comprehension.question: How is Nora using numbers, and how many shapes does she count altogether?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She is counting shapes on a chart, and she counts fifty shapes altogether.
comprehension.explanation: The text says Nora counts 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, so her final total is fifty.

comprehension.question: How far did Leo jump?
comprehension.explanation: The text says Leo is measuring how far he can jump, and gives the exact number in centimetres.

comprehension.question: The last sentence gives three ways we use numbers. What are they?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Numbers help us count, measure and tell the time.
comprehension.explanation: Each of the three children shows one of the three uses, and the last sentence lists them together.

comprehension.question: What did people use to measure long ago in Egypt, and what did they use in Rome?
comprehension.correctAnswer: In Egypt they measured with fingers, hands and arms. In Rome they measured in footsteps.
comprehension.explanation: The text names one method for each place, so a full answer keeps the two apart.

comprehension.question: Why does the text say a ruler is better than measuring with our own feet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because a ruler and a metre stick are the same for everyone, but feet are all different sizes.
comprehension.explanation: The last sentence gives the reason: the tool is the same for everyone, so the answer never changes.

comprehension.question: What did the children in the story want to make, and how long did it need to be?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A new reading corner, with a mat long enough for six friends to sit on.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin explains the plan in her first speech, including how many friends the mat must hold.

comprehension.question: Why did Amal and Leo get different answers when they measured the floor with their feet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Leo's feet are big and Amal's feet are small, so the same floor took a different number of feet.
comprehension.explanation: Nora spots the reason by looking down at their feet, and explains it to both of them.

comprehension.question: What did Teacher Yasmin say they should use instead, and how many centimetres are in one metre?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A ruler and a metre stick, and there are one hundred centimetres in one metre.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin names the two tools, then gives the number when she hands over the metre stick.

comprehension.question: Which basket was heavy, and who ran to help carry it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The big basket of cushions was heavy, and Maya ran to help Sami carry it.
comprehension.explanation: Sami lifts the big basket and calls it heavy; Maya joins him so the load is fair for both of them.

comprehension.question: Daniel held up two ribbons. Which ribbon did the children need for the big cushion, and why?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The red ribbon, because it was longer than the green one.
comprehension.explanation: Daniel compares the two ribbons, calling the red one longer and the green one shorter.

comprehension.question: Why did Amal say it was not fair for Theo to sit down first, and what did Theo do next?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Being tall does not mean you go first. Theo thought about it, said sorry and made room for everyone.
comprehension.explanation: Amal answers Theo gently rather than crossly, and Theo changes his mind straight afterwards.

comprehension.question: Teacher Yasmin said, “A fair measure is the same for everyone.” What do you think she meant?
comprehension.correctAnswer: That a good measure, such as a metre, never changes from person to person, so nobody is treated unfairly.
comprehension.explanation: Link the answer back to the feet at the start of the story, which changed with every child.

comprehension.question: The children measured the floor slowly and carefully. Tell about a time when you had to be patient like that. What happened?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept any personal example that names the task, says what the child did to stay patient, and says how it ended.
comprehension.explanation: This question links the story to the child's own life, so ask them to compare their patience with the children's.

grammar.title: Comparing Two Things (-er than)
grammar.explanation: When we measure, we usually want to say which of two things is bigger, longer, taller or heavier. To compare two things we add -er to the describing word, and then we use the word than.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Take a short describing word, add -er, then add than.
big becomes bigger: “An elephant is bigger than a goat.”
long becomes longer: “This ribbon is longer than that ribbon.”
tall becomes taller: “Theo is taller than Leo.”
small becomes smaller: “A mouse is smaller than a cat.”
Two spellings change a little. In big the g doubles, so we write bigger. In heavy the y turns into i, so we write heavier.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “This line is more long than that line.” Short describing words take -er instead of more, so the sentence becomes “This line is longer than that line.”
grammar.memoryTip: Say “-er than” as one little song: bigg-er than, long-er than, tall-er than.
grammar.practice:
Add -er to the word in brackets and finish each sentence.
1. (long) A snake is ______ than a worm.
2. (tall) A tree is ______ than a bush.
3. (big) An elephant is ______ than a mouse.
4. (small) A pebble is ______ than a rock.
5. (heavy) A big basket is ______ than a small basket.

grammar.title: Comparing Two Things: Measure and Compare
grammar.explanation: Now you choose the two things yourself. A strong comparing sentence names both things, uses one describing word ending in -er, and joins them with than, so your reader knows exactly what you measured.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Name the first thing, add the -er word, say than, then name the second thing.
My pencil is longer than my rubber.
The metre stick is heavier than the ruler.
The door is taller than the window.
The path is narrower than the road.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to forget the second thing and write “My pencil is longer.” The reader then asks, longer than what? Write both things: “My pencil is longer than my rubber.”
grammar.memoryTip: Check three things every time: which two objects, which -er word, and did I remember than?
grammar.practice: Choose five pairs of objects at home. Measure or look carefully at each pair, then write one comparing sentence for every pair. Use a different -er word each time, taken from bigger, longer, taller, heavier, wider and higher.

grammar.title: How Many? There is and There are
grammar.explanation: When we count, we ask “How many…?” to find out the number of things. We answer with There is for one thing, and with There are for two or more things.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
One thing takes is: “There is one sun in the sky.”
Two or more things take are: “There are ten circles on the chart.”
To ask the question we say: “How many children are there?”
To answer we say: “There are twenty children.”
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “There is ten circles.” Ten is more than one, so the sentence becomes “There are ten circles.”
grammar.memoryTip: One equals is. More than one equals are. If you can count more than one, choose are.
grammar.practice:
Write is or are in each gap.
1. There ______ twenty children in the class.
2. There ______ one clock on the wall.
3. How many shapes ______ there?
4. There ______ fifty beans in the jar.
5. There ______ one teacher in the room.

grammar.title: How Many? Make Your Own Survey
grammar.explanation: A survey asks the same question about several different things and writes down every answer. In this task you ask “How many…?” about your own home, and you answer in a full There is or There are sentence.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Ask, count, then answer in a whole sentence.
How many doors are there? There are four doors.
How many clocks are there? There is one clock.
How many windows are there? There are seven windows.
How many tables are there? There is one table.
grammar.commonMistake: A one-word answer such as “Four.” hides the pattern you are practising. Write the whole sentence instead: “There are four doors.”
grammar.memoryTip: Count first, then look at your number. Any number bigger than one always calls for are.
grammar.practice: Choose six things in your home, such as doors, chairs, windows, cups, shoes and clocks. Write the question “How many ______ are there?” for each one, count carefully, and write a full There is or There are answer underneath every question.

grammar.title: Words That Sound the Same
grammar.explanation: Some words sound exactly the same but mean different things and are spelled differently. Four of these pairs matter in this unit about numbers, and we must pick the right word whenever we write.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
one is the number 1, and won means that you win a game: “I won one race.”
two is the number 2, and too means also: “I have two books, and you have two, too.”
four is the number 4, and for means given to: “These four mangoes are for you.”
eight is the number 8, and ate is the past form of eat: “I ate breakfast at eight o'clock.”
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “I have to apples.” The word needed here is the number, so the sentence becomes “I have two apples.”
grammar.memoryTip: Read the whole sentence before you choose. If the word tells you how many, it is the number word: one, two, four or eight.
grammar.practice:
Choose the correct word for each gap.
1. I have ______ (two / too) hands.
2. She ______ (eight / ate) all her rice.
3. These sweets are ______ (four / for) my friends.
4. My team ______ (one / won) the game!
5. You can come ______ (two / too).

grammar.title: Words That Sound the Same: Choose and Check
grammar.explanation: A careful writer checks every word that has a sound-alike partner. In this task you write your own sentence for each word in the pair, so the difference in meaning becomes clear on the page.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Write one sentence for each word in the pair.
one: I have one metre stick. won: Our team won the counting game.
two: There are two ribbons. too: Maya wants a ribbon too.
four: The square has four sides. for: This heart card is for Nora.
eight: The clock says eight. ate: Leo ate his lunch quickly.
grammar.commonMistake: Swapping the pair inside a sentence, as in “Our team one the game.”, changes the meaning completely. Read the sentence aloud and ask what the word is doing before you choose.
grammar.memoryTip: Cover the gap and say the sentence. If the gap needs a number, use one, two, four or eight; if it does not, use the partner word.
grammar.practice: Write eight sentences of your own, one for every word in the four pairs: one and won, two and too, four and for, eight and ate. Underline the sound-alike word in each sentence, then read your sentences to an adult and check them together.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Count in Tens
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Find small objects such as beans, stones or bottle caps, and ask an adult to sit with you.
Put the objects into groups of ten. Point to each group as you say: “Ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety, one hundred!”
Now say the same numbers backwards, from one hundred down to ten.
Record yourself. Check: did you say every number clearly, and did you keep them all in the right order?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, count in tens to one hundred for the tutor and ask it to name any number that sounded unclear.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Name and Describe Shapes
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Draw or find these five shapes: a circle, a square, a triangle, a rectangle and a heart.
Hold up each shape and say the whole sentence, for example “This is a triangle.” All five shape names begin with a consonant sound, so every one of them takes a and not an.
Then say how many sides it has, for example “A square has four sides.”
Finish by naming the shape in something around you, for example “I can see a circle in the clock face.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Describe one shape to the tutor without saying its name, while an adult listens, and ask the tutor to guess which shape you mean.

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Bigger, Longer, Taller
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this comparing game with an adult or a friend.
Choose two things that you can both see, for example two pencils or two books.
Say one comparing sentence about them, such as “This pencil is longer than that pencil.”
Swap turns and change the describing word every round: bigger than, taller than, heavier than, wider than. Keep playing until you have used six different -er words.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name two objects, then say a comparing sentence about them out loud while your helper checks that you used than.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Measurement Detective
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Be a measurement detective in your own home, with an adult helping you hold the ruler.
Choose five objects and measure each one with a ruler or a metre stick, starting at the zero mark every time.
Say each result in a full sentence, for example “The table is ninety centimetres long.”
Record your five sentences, then listen back and check that you said both the number and the unit every time.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read one of your measuring sentences to the tutor with an adult nearby, and ask it whether centimetres or metres suits that object better.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Shape and Pattern Talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Make a repeating pattern from things you already have, such as bottle caps, leaves or buttons.
Point along your pattern and describe it out loud: “It goes circle, square, circle, square.”
Then say what comes next and why: “The next one is a circle, because the pattern repeats.”
Record your description and play it to a family member. Ask them to guess the shape that comes after the part you described.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Say your repeating pattern to the tutor while an adult sits with you, then ask it to continue the pattern for three more steps.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - How Many Survey
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Present a short survey of your home to your class.
Choose four things to count, such as doors, windows, chairs and cups.
For each one, ask your question aloud and then answer it: “How many windows are there? There are seven windows.”
Record your whole survey, speak slowly, and check that you chose There is for one thing and There are for more than one.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Practise your four survey questions with the tutor first, with an adult beside you, and ask it to suggest one extra thing worth counting.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Numbers in Words
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these three sentences with a number written in words, then write two more sentences of your own that each use a number word.
1. There are ______ children in my class.
2. I can count to ______.
3. My house has ______ windows.
writing.modelText: There are thirty children in my class. I can count to one hundred. My house has six windows. There are twenty books on our shelf. I have ten fingers and ten toes.
writing.successCriteria: Every number is written in words, every sentence begins with a capital letter, and every sentence ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Copy the spelling of each number word from Group 1 of your word list before you write it.
writing.extension: Write one more sentence that compares two of your numbers, such as “Thirty is bigger than twenty.”

writing.title: Writing 2 — Describe and Compare
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose two objects in your home. Write two comparing sentences about them, one using bigger and one using longer. Then draw both objects and label which one is bigger.
writing.modelText: The metre stick is longer than my ruler. The blue box is bigger than the red box.
writing.successCriteria: Both sentences name two objects, use a describing word ending in -er, and include the word than.
writing.support: Say each sentence out loud first, using the “-er than” song from Grammar Lesson 1.
writing.extension: Add two more comparing sentences that use taller and heavier.

writing.title: Story response: A Fair Way to Measure
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer three tasks about “A Fair Way to Measure”. First write two sentences about who is tall and who is short in the story. Next write one sentence about something big and one about something heavy. Finally draw the new reading corner and write one caption under your picture.
writing.modelText:
Theo is the tallest child in the class. Nora is shorter than Theo.
The big basket holds all of the cushions. The basket of cushions is heavy for one child.
The six friends sit together on their new mat under the shady tree.
writing.successCriteria: I used four measuring words from this unit, named the characters correctly, and wrote one clear caption for my drawing.
writing.support: Reread the last five paragraphs of the story before you write your caption.
writing.extension: Write what Theo might say to Amal on the following morning.

writing.title: Writing 4 - My Measurement Table
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make a table with three columns headed Object, Length and Unit. Measure five things in your home with a ruler or a metre stick, and fill in one row for each object. Underneath the table, write two sentences about what you found.
writing.modelText:
Object: my pencil. Length: 15. Unit: centimetres.
Object: the kitchen table. Length: 1. Unit: metre.
My pencil is fifteen centimetres long. The kitchen table is one metre long.
writing.successCriteria: Every row names the object, the number and the unit, and both sentences write the number in words.
writing.support: Use centimetres for small things and metres for long things.
writing.extension: Add a fourth column headed Longer or shorter than my ruler, and tick the right answer for every object.

writing.title: Writing 5 - Explain a Pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw a pattern that repeats at least three times, using shapes or colours. Then write three sentences: one that names the shapes in your pattern, one that says what comes next, and one that explains how you know.
writing.modelText: My pattern goes circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle. The next shape is a circle. I know this because the three shapes repeat in the same order every time.
writing.successCriteria: My three sentences name the pattern, predict the next part, and explain my thinking using the word because.
writing.support: Say your pattern out loud twice before you write it down.
writing.extension: Make a second pattern that repeats two colours and two shapes at the same time, then describe it.

writing.title: Writing 6 - A Fair Way to Share
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short set of instructions that tells a friend how to share ten counters fairly between two people. Number your steps, and use a “There are…” sentence to say how many counters each person receives.
writing.modelText:
1. Count all the counters. There are ten counters.
2. Put one counter in front of your friend.
3. Put one counter in front of yourself.
4. Keep going until no counters are left.
5. Count each pile. There are five counters in each pile.
writing.successCriteria: My steps are numbered and in order, each one begins with an action word, and I used There are correctly twice.
writing.support: Do the sharing with real counters first, then write down exactly what you did.
writing.extension: Write a second set of steps that shares twelve counters between three people.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Fill in the Blanks (Counting in Tens)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the missing number words on the lines.
1. Ten, ______, thirty, ______, fifty.
2. Sixty, ______, eighty, ______, one hundred.
3. There are ______ tens in one hundred.

activities.title: Activity 2 — True or False (Shape Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each fact. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
1. A circle has four sides.
2. A square has four equal sides.
3. A triangle has three corners.
4. A rectangle has three sides.
activities.answerSummary: 1. False, because a circle has no sides at all. 2. True. 3. True. 4. False, because a rectangle has four sides.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Match the Shape to the Object
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Draw a line from each shape to the object that has that shape.
Shapes: circle, square, triangle, rectangle.
Objects: a door, a window, the sun, a slice of watermelon.
activities.answerSummary: circle → the sun, square → a window, triangle → a slice of watermelon, rectangle → a door.

activities.title: Activity 4 — Compare and Write
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Look at each pair and write one comparing sentence with -er than.
1. an elephant and a mouse (big)
2. a snake and a worm (long)
3. a big stone and a small stone (heavy)
4. a tree and a bush (tall)
activities.answerSummary: 1. An elephant is bigger than a mouse. 2. A snake is longer than a worm. 3. A big stone is heavier than a small stone. 4. A tree is taller than a bush.

activities.title: Activity 5 - Estimate, Measure, Check
activities.instructionsAndItems: Choose six things in your home and make a table with three columns headed Guess, Real length and How close. For each object, first guess how long it is in centimetres and write your guess. Then measure it with a ruler and write the real number. Finally write whether your guess was too big, too small or very close.
activities.answerSummary: Answers depend on the objects chosen. Check that every row has a guess in centimetres, a measured number in centimetres, and a comment that compares the two numbers.

activities.title: Activity 6 - Shape Hunt
activities.instructionsAndItems: Go on a shape hunt around your home. Find at least two objects for each of these shapes: circle, square, triangle, rectangle and heart. Write the objects under the right heading. Then answer one question for every shape, writing out the whole sentence, for example “How many circles did you find? There are four circles.”
activities.answerSummary: The objects will vary. Check that every shape heading has at least two objects, and that each answer is written as a full There is or There are sentence with the correct number.

assignments.title: Let's Measure learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work. First complete “Writing 4 - My Measurement Table”, measuring five objects and writing each one into your table with its unit. Next record “Speaking 1 — Count in Tens”, counting up to one hundred and then all the way back down. Finally complete “Writing 2 — Describe and Compare” and check that both sentences use an -er word together with than. Read all three pieces against the writing and speaking rubrics before you submit them together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: How many tens are there in one hundred?
quizzes.explanation: One hundred is made of ten groups of ten, so the answer is ten. Five, twenty and one hundred are all real numbers, but none of them counts the tens.

quizzes.question: Which number word comes straight after seventy when you count in tens?
quizzes.explanation: Counting in tens runs seventy, eighty, ninety. Sixty comes earlier, seventeen is not a ten, and ninety comes one step later.

quizzes.question: Which shape has no corners and no straight sides?
quizzes.explanation: A circle is completely round. The other three shapes are all built from straight sides that meet at corners.

quizzes.question: How many sides does a triangle have?
quizzes.explanation: A triangle has three straight sides and three corners. A square and a rectangle each have four sides instead.

quizzes.question: Look at this pattern: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle. Which shape comes next?
quizzes.explanation: The three shapes repeat in the same order, so after triangle the pattern begins again with circle.

quizzes.question: How many centimetres are there in one metre?
quizzes.explanation: Teacher Yasmin tells the class that one metre is always one hundred centimetres long.

quizzes.question: Which sentence compares the two animals correctly?
quizzes.options: A snake is longer than a worm. | A snake is more long than a worm. | A snake is long than a worm. | A snake is longest than a worm.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A snake is longer than a worm.
quizzes.explanation: Short describing words take -er rather than more, and a comparison of two things always needs than after the -er word.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses is or are correctly?
quizzes.options: There is ten circles on the chart. | There are one clock on the wall. | There are ten circles on the chart. | There is many circles on the chart.
quizzes.correctAnswer: There are ten circles on the chart.
quizzes.explanation: Ten is more than one, so the sentence needs are. There is only fits a single thing, such as one clock.

quizzes.question: Choose the correct word: “I have ______ hands.”
quizzes.explanation: Two is the number 2. Too means also, to is a joining word, and ate is the past form of eat.

quizzes.question: What does the word weight mean?
quizzes.options: How long something is from end to end. | A design that repeats again and again. | A flat tool for drawing straight lines. | How heavy or how light something is.
quizzes.correctAnswer: How heavy or how light something is.
quizzes.explanation: Weight tells us how heavy something feels. The other three options describe length, a pattern and a ruler.

liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and counting chant; 10 min grouping beans into tens; 10 min naming the five shapes on cards; 15 min Speaking 2 in pairs; 5 min reflection
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 1 — Count in Tens and upload it.

liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Grammar 4 and bring your five comparing sentences.

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The prompts below are drawn from the source pack, grouped by file. Vocabulary: “Can you help me practise counting in tens?”, “How do I write these numbers in numerals and in words?”, “What is the difference between a square and a rectangle?”, “When do I use centimetres and when do I use metres?”, “How do I compare sizes in English?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “Why do we say longer but more colourful?”, “Can you give me five more sentences to practise is and are?” Lesson: “Can you read the text with me and explain the hard words?”, “Can you play a counting-in-tens listening game with me?”, “What is the difference between fifteen and fifty?”, “What is the difference between long and tall?” Story: “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?”, “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”

Lay out real measuring tools before every session: one ruler per pair, one metre stick for the class, and small objects such as beans or bottle caps for counting in tens. Watch for two common slips. Children often start measuring from the very end of the ruler instead of the zero mark, so model the correct start each time. They also confuse fifteen with fifty and sixteen with sixty, so stress the ending as the class counts. When marking comparing sentences, treat a missing than as the next step rather than as a mistake, and praise a correct -er spelling out loud. A child with no ruler at home can measure in hand spans and footsteps, which is exactly what the story asks everyone to think about. Mark the unit portfolio out of 32: award 16 marks for the two writing pieces against rub-writing-v1, checking that the measurement table gives a unit in every row, and 16 marks for the counting recording against rub-speaking-v1, checking that the child counts up and then back down without skipping a ten. The story carries the unit's value: measure carefully, be patient, and share fairly.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. twenty, forty. 2. seventy, ninety. 3. ten, because one hundred is ten groups of ten.
1. False; a circle has no sides at all. 2. True. 3. True. 4. False; a rectangle has four sides. Both corrections must be written out in full.
circle → the sun, square → a window, triangle → a slice of watermelon, rectangle → a door.
1. An elephant is bigger than a mouse. 2. A snake is longer than a worm. 3. A big stone is heavier than a small stone. 4. A tree is taller than a bush.
1. longer. 2. taller. 3. bigger, with a double g. 4. smaller. 5. heavier, because the y turns into i.
1. are. 2. is. 3. are. 4. are. 5. is.
1. two. 2. ate. 3. for. 4. won. 5. too.
The sun is a circle and a book is a rectangle. Both parts are needed for the mark.
Circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle. The order matters, so mark it wrong if the shapes are swapped.
One mark for describing the things we see, and one mark for making patterns with shapes.
Fingers. Accept little fingers or children's fingers.
One hundred. Accept the numeral 100 as well as the words.
Ten children. Award the mark if the child explains that ten tens make one hundred.
She is counting shapes on a chart, and the total is fifty. Both halves are needed.
Forty-five centimetres. The unit must be given, not just the number.
Count, measure and tell the time. All three are needed for the mark.
Egypt: fingers, hands and arms. Rome: footsteps. Do not accept the two places being swapped.
Because a ruler is the same for everyone, while feet come in many different sizes.
A new reading corner, with a mat long enough for six friends. Accept a mat for six children.
Leo's feet are big and Amal's feet are small, so each of them counted a different number of feet.
A ruler and a metre stick, and one metre holds one hundred centimetres.
The big basket of cushions, and Maya ran to help Sami with it.
The red ribbon, because Daniel says it is longer than the green one.
Being tall does not decide who goes first. Theo agreed, said sorry, and made room so everyone sat together.
A metre never changes, so everyone gets the same answer. Look for a link back to the feet at the start of the story.
Personal answer. Award the mark when the child names the task, one thing they did to stay patient, and the result.
ten. One hundred is ten groups of ten.
eighty. In tens the order runs seventy, eighty, ninety.
circle. It is the only shape in the list without corners.
three. A triangle has three sides and three corners.
circle. The three shapes repeat, so the pattern starts again.
Three completed sentences plus two of the child's own, with every number written in words rather than as a numeral. Any sensible number is correct.
Five comparing sentences, each naming two objects and using a different -er word with than. Reject any sentence that stops after the -er word.
Six questions in the form How many ______ are there?, each with a full answer. Answers of one thing take There is; answers of two or more take There are.
Eight sentences, one for each of one, won, two, too, four, for, eight and ate, with the sound-alike word underlined in every sentence.
Listen for five measured objects. Each spoken sentence must contain the object, the number and the unit, for example ninety centimetres.
The recording must name the repeating unit of the pattern, say what comes next, and give a reason that uses the word because.
Four question-and-answer pairs. Check that a count of one takes There is and a count above one takes There are.
Five table rows, each with an object, a number and a unit, plus two sentences that write the measurement in words.
Three sentences: the pattern named in order, the next part predicted, and an explanation that uses because.
Five numbered steps in a workable order, each starting with an action word, including two correct There are sentences.
Six rows, each with a guess in centimetres, a measured length in centimetres, and a comment saying too big, too small or very close.
At least two objects under each of the five shape headings, and five full sentences that begin There is or There are.
one hundred. One metre always holds one hundred centimetres.
A snake is longer than a worm. Short words take -er, never more.
There are ten circles on the chart. Ten is more than one, so are is needed.
two. It is the number word, while too means also.
How heavy or how light something is.

selfAssessment.statement: I can count in tens from ten to one hundred.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name and describe the shapes we learned.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can notice a pattern and say what comes next.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use measuring words like long, tall and heavy.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can compare two things using bigger, longer and taller.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask “How many?” and answer with “There are…”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level3: Mostly clear and easy to follow
Most number, shape and measuring words used correctly
rubrics.level4: Wide range of unit words used naturally
-er, than, is and are always correct

rubrics.level1: Needs full support to take a turn
rubrics.level4: Takes turns, listens and adds a new idea

rubrics.level1: Ideas unclear or away from the task
rubrics.level4: Clear ideas with well chosen detail

rubrics.level2: Some order, with gaps in the sequence
rubrics.level4: Strong order using numbered steps or a clear table

rubrics.level3: Good use of number, shape and measuring words
rubrics.level4: Precise word choices with the correct unit every time

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops mostly missing
rubrics.level2: Some capital letters and full stops
rubrics.level3: Capital letters, spaces and full stops usually correct
